look_at.text: Chip is completely sealed off. The Sony WH-1000XM5s are clamped to his head, eyes tracking all four screens simultaneously. He does not register my presence in the slightest.
Chip sits at the centre of a four-monitor command post. His eyes move between screens with the focus of someone who finds people a significant step down from servers.

talk_to.text: The XM5s have created a sealed ecosystem of one. He can't hear a thing. He's mouthing something — FIFA strategy, probably.

give.text: I try to get his attention. Nothing. The headphones have made him unreachable.

effects.text: Chip's eyes lock onto the headphones.
Are those— those are the Sony WH-1000XM5s. I've been looking for those for three months.
He grabs them without ceremony and puts them on, while tossing his old one. His expression shifts immediately.
Oh. Oh that's... these are leagues better than mine.
These are staying on forever.
Eyes forward, head nodding slightly. You no longer exist to him.
You hold out the headphones. Chip clocks them instantly.
Those are mine.
He takes them, puts them on, and freezes.
Wait. These are the XM5s. My old ones are garbage compared to these.
He pulls his old headphones off the hook and tosses them in the bin without looking. The world outside his monitors ceases to matter.

combinations.text: You wave the egg at him. He's completely sealed off in his headphone bubble. He does not react. The egg and Chip regard each other. Only one of them knows this is happening.

effects.text: You hold out the frozen egg. Chip glances at it without turning his head.
Why do you have an egg.
Found it in the fridge.
Put it back.
He turns back to his monitors. Exchange concluded.

combinations.text: You hold the frozen egg in his eyeline. Nothing. The headphones have made him bulletproof.

effects.text: You extend the frozen egg toward Chip. He clocks it without turning his head.
I don't want that. Whatever it is. No.

combinations.text: You offer the damp egg to a man sealed inside noise-cancelling headphones. It does not go well for the egg's dignity.

effects.text: You offer Chip the slightly damp, faintly coffee-stained egg.
Is that an egg.
Yes.
Why is it wet.
Coffee.
Chip stares at you for a long, uncomfortable moment.
I don't want it. I really don't want it.

combinations.text: You wave the cup near him. He can't hear you. He might be able to smell it though — his nose wrinkles slightly.

effects.text: You offer Chip the cup of filter coffee.
I can smell that from here.
That's from the kitchen machine. I would rather drink directly from a motivational poster.
He waves you off without looking up.

combinations.text: You hold the book up to his eyeline. He can't hear you. He can, however, read the cover. His jaw tightens almost imperceptibly.

effects.text: You hold out 'Winning Ugly'. Chip clocks the cover mid-keystroke.
I've read that.
Oh.
Chapter nine is correct. Don't tell anyone I said that.
He goes back to typing. The book is still yours.

combinations.text: You hold the package in front of him. He can't hear you. His eyes flick to the label for one frame, then back to his monitors. Something in his expression does not change.

effects.text: You show Chip the package. He reads the label.
That's addressed to Chen.
Yes.
I revoked their access Tuesday. 9:47 AM.
A beat.
Take it to HR. Assuming they exist.

combinations.text: You hold the Jeff Bezos business card in front of Chip's face. He can't hear you. He reads it. His expression does not change. He looks back at his monitors.

effects.text: You hold up the Jeff Bezos business card.
Jeff Bezos.
Yes.
He's never submitted a ticket either.
He turns back to his screens.

combinations.text: You offer the stress ball to a man inside noise-cancelling headphones. He cannot receive it. He does not have stress anyway. He has tickets.

effects.text: You hold out the Amazon stress ball.
I don't have stress.
I have six open tickets. Those are categorically different.
He slides it back without looking at it.

combinations.text: You hold up the USB stick. Chip clocks the label from behind his headphones. A flicker of recognition. Those aren't supposed to leave the lab. He looks back at his monitors. Not his ticket queue.

effects.text: You hold out the USB stick. Chip reads the label.
That's a CVNA build token.
Yes.
Those are not supposed to leave the lab.
It was just sitting on a workstation.
Yes. That is also a policy violation.
A pause.
Not my ticket queue.
He looks away. The token remains yours.
You hold out the separation agreement.
That's not an IT issue.
I know, I just—
Log a ticket with HR. Three to five business weeks.
You present the signed agreement to Chip.
I don't countersign things. I don't have signing permissions. I have root access to three servers and a very clean commit history.
You offer Chip the pen.
I use a keyboard.
He says this like it explains everything. It does.
You hold up the visitor badge.
You already have physical access.
I just thought—
Badge provisioning is not IT. Badge provisioning is facilities. Different ticket system.
You offer the cappuccino. Chip doesn't look up.
I don't drink cappuccinos.
Why not?
Milk interferes with cognitive function. There are studies.
That seems made up.
Everything I do is evidence-based.

look_at.text: Four monitors. Left: a build terminal, logs scrolling. Second: Phone Tool — the employee directory. I scan straight for the cost center column. There it is: Computer Vision North America (CVNA) lab — 2847. Third: support tickets, 112 unread. Far right: a budget spreadsheet. I have what I came for.

Four monitors, all live. Left: a build terminal. Second: something called Phone Tool — looks like an employee directory with cost center columns. Third: a support ticket system, 11 unread. Far right: a budget spreadsheet. There's a lot here. I'm not sure what I'm looking for.
Chip's workstation. Four monitors, all active. I can't really look at the screens without him noticing.

use.text: With Chip sealed off in his headphone bubble, I lean in and check the Phone Tool on screen two. Cost center column... CVNA lab — 2847. That's the one.
I can get close enough to read the screens, but there's a lot of data across four monitors. I don't know what I'm supposed to be looking for.

look_at.text: Chip's own headphones, hanging unused on the monitor. This is a man so confident in his focus that he doesn't even need them. A power move.

pick_up.text: These are Chip's. Taking them while he's watching would not go well.

use.text: I don't need to use the headphones here.

look_at.text: A humming server rack, every port lit, every cable zip-tied with the care of someone who considers this their life's work. A handwritten label reads: 'DO NOT UNPLUG — ANY OF IT — EVER — Chip'.

use.text: I cannot get there - way is blocked.

open.text: I cannot get there - way is blocked. A sticker on the latch anyway reads: 'If you have to ask, the answer is no.'

look_at.text: A corkboard pinned with printed screenshots: terrible support tickets. 'MY COMPUTER IS THINKING TOO LOUD.' 'THE INTERNET IS RUNNING OUT.' 'EXCEL SAYS IT DOESN'T KNOW WHAT I WANT.' Each one has a date. Some have red stars. Chip has a rating system.

use.text: The Wall of Shame judges me. I shouldn't interfere.

look_at.text: A stack of old CRT monitors, four deep. The top one still has a sticky note: 'WORKS FINE — DO NOT RECYCLE'. The second one says the same thing. So does the third.

use.text: I can't do anything with a pile of old monitors.

pick_up.text: They're older than I am and heavier.

look_at.text: A carefully constructed pyramid of empty energy drink cans. Eight cans. This took effort. This took pride. A small laminated sign reads: 'DO NOT TOUCH — Chip'.

use.text: I'm not touching Chip's pyramid. I have a sense of self-preservation.

pick_up.text: The sign says do not touch. I believe the sign.

look_at.text: You head back to the office.

open.text: You head back to the office.

use.text: You head back to the office.
